candidate_paradigm.primary_mechanism: Population P = {entity_i, i=1..N} with heritable trait z_i ∈ ℝ^k (or any abelian group) and Darwinian fitness w_i ∈ ℝ_≥0 = expected offspring count of entity i. Population-mean trait E[z] := (Σ_i p_i z_i) where p_i = frequency-weight (uniform 1/N or measure-theoretic dp). Mean fitness w̄ := E[w_i] = Σ_i p_i w_i. Parent-to-offspring trait change Δz_i := z_i' - z_i where z_i' = mean trait of i's offspring (encompasses mutation, recombination, cultural transmission bias, environmental modification). PRICE EQUATION (Price 1970 Nature 227:520, derived from 1-step direct algebraic expansion): w̄ · ΔE[z] = Cov(w_i, z_i) + E[w_i · Δz_i] where Cov(w_i, z_i) = E[w_i · z_i] - E[w_i] · E[z_i] = (1/N) Σ_i (w_i - w̄)(z_i - E[z]) and E[w_i · Δz_i] = Σ_i p_i w_i Δz_i. INTERPRETATION: LEFT side = (mean fitness) × (change in mean trait from parent to offspring generation); FIRST term on right = COVARIANCE(fitness, trait) capturing differential reproduction (selection); SECOND term = fitness-weighted average within-lineage trait change (transmission/drift/mutation). MULTILEVEL/HIERARCHICAL extension via nested partition (Price 1972; recursion-of-self): given partition of population into groups {g}, with W_g = mean fitness in group g, Z_g = mean trait in group g, the equation recursively decomposes: w̄ · ΔE[z] = Cov_groups(W_g, Z_g) + E_groups[Cov_individuals|g(w_i, z_i)] + E_groups[E_individuals|g[w_i · Δz_i]] = BETWEEN-GROUP selection + WITHIN-GROUP selection + transmission-bias. THREE EQUIVALENT FORMS: (i) DISCRETE-GENERATION POPULATION: w̄ · ΔE[z] = Cov(w_i, z_i) + E[w_i Δz_i]; (ii) CONTINUOUS-TIME REPLICATOR LIMIT: dE[z]/dt = Cov(f_i, z_i)/E[f_i] + E[f_i · ż_i] where f_i = malthusian fitness ż = dz_i/dt (continuous-time Hofbauer-Sigmund 1998 Cambridge); (iii) MEASURE-THEORETIC GENERAL FORM (Kerr-Godfrey-Smith 2009 Phil. Sci.): ∫ z dμ' - ∫ z dμ = ∫ (z - z̄)(w/w̄) dμ + ∫ (w/w̄) Δz dμ for general probability measure μ on type-space. SPECIAL CASES: (a) ROBERTSON 1966 secondary theorem of natural selection: assume Δz_i = 0 (perfect transmission, no mutation/drift), then ΔE[z] = Cov(w_i, z_i)/w̄ — selection-only response; (b) HAMILTON'S RULE 1964/1975 inclusive-fitness derivation: write w_i = a_i + b·r_ij·s_j (own fitness + benefit b weighted by relatedness r and recipient social trait s_j), then Cov(w_i, z_i) decomposes into Cov(a_i, z_i) + b · E[r·s · z] yielding rb > c criterion; (c) BREEDER'S EQUATION quantitative genetics: assume z = additive-genetic-value g + noise e, then R = ΔE[z] = h² · S where h² = heritability = Var(g)/Var(z), S = selection differential = Cov(w, z)/w̄ (Lush 1937; Falconer-Mackay 1996); (d) FUNDAMENTAL THEOREM OF NATURAL SELECTION (Fisher 1930 reinterpreted via Price): the rate of fitness increase due to selection equals the additive genetic variance in fitness V_A(w)/w̄. EXAMPLES: (i) BACTERIAL POPULATION under antibiotic — z = MIC (minimum inhibitory concentration), w = survival × growth; Cov(w, z) > 0 ⟹ MIC increases (resistance evolves); (ii) FINCH BEAK SIZE during drought (Grant-Grant 1986 Galápagos) — z = beak depth, w = seed-cracking survival; Cov(w, z) explicitly measured; (iii) GROUP SELECTION in social spiders (Pruitt-Goodnight 2014) — between-group Cov(W_g, Z_g) for boldness trait Z_g; (iv) ALGORITHMIC: genetic algorithm fitness-proportional selection — Price equation predicts trait-mean change exactly per generation; (v) TUMOR clonal expansion — z = driver-mutation count, w = clonal proliferation rate; Cov(w, z) measures positive selection. CONSEQUENCES: foundational identity for KIN SELECTION (Hamilton 1964/1975), GROUP SELECTION (Wilson 1975, Sober-Wilson 1998), MULTILEVEL SELECTION (Okasha 2006), CULTURAL EVOLUTION (Boyd-Richerson 1985 dual-inheritance), QUANTITATIVE GENETICS (Lande-Arnold 1983 selection-gradient), EVOLUTIONARY-DYNAMICS replicator equations (Hofbauer-Sigmund 1998), NICHE CONSTRUCTION (Odling-Smee-Laland-Feldman 2003).

CONTINUOUS-TIME REPLICATOR-DYNAMICS limit: dx_i/dt = x_i (f_i - f̄) where x_i = type-i frequency, f_i = malthusian fitness, f̄ = mean fitness; derived as continuous-time limit of Price equation when generations overlap and fitness differentials are small (Hofbauer-Sigmund 1998 'Evolutionary Games and Population Dynamics' Cambridge)
